publicationStmt.p: Transcribed from Bodleian Library, Lovelace Byron collection, Box 166, folios 159-166. Transcription by Lisa Gold (2022). TEI conversion for Sextant pipeline, 2026.

div.p: St James' Sqre Friday. 29th Novr (2 o'clock).
My Dear Crow. I feel rather disappointed at not hearing from you this morning. I wonder why you did not write me a line, & yet I have no business to expect daily communications from you.
I have had Wheatstone with me the last 5 hours. He is just gone. He is going to Paris on Monday & will be back in a month.
He will visit me at Ockham in Jany. He has given me much important information, & still more important advice. He is anxious I

should take such a position as may enable me to influence Prince Albert, who is (he knows) a very clever young man. John Crosse told me the same thing, which he knew from the first-rate men of science in Germany. The Prince's Whole & sole desire was to be at the head of a scientific circle in England, & he has expressed his utter mortification at the opposition & cold water which have been thrown on all his desires in this respect.
Wheatstone says none but some woman can put him in the right way, & open the door to him towards all he desires; & that a woman can say that which any man would get into
a scrape by doing. Wheatstone does not wish me to think of doing anything immediate.

By no means. But he says it would occur in the natural course of things that if I can take a certain standing in the course of the next few years, the Prince would on some occasion speak to me about science; and that in that case, if I happily seize the moment, I may do for science an inestimable benefit; for that all the Prince wants is a sensible adviser & suggester, to indicate to him the channels for his exercising a scientific influence.
He is very clever they say, but in a slow way; not a brilliant man.
All this is very curious; & it is odd enough that both John Crosse & Wheatstone should have given me the same account within a week, tho' derived from very different sources.

Oddly enough too, Wheatstone has spoken to me of the great importance of studying the German philosophy & science. Altogether my visit to Broomfield seems to have been very opportune, & to have laid the foundations for much that is wanted for me just at this epoch of my progress.
I am glad however that I stuck to my original day for leaving it. I should not have derived much more by staying there a few days longer at the present time.

2. As far as the German studies are concerned, they cannot be carried on while upon a visit; & it is at home only that I should make any real progress in such. If young Crosse stays with us, some hours can be daily given for the time-being, to that purpose; but when one is visiting a family one must be diluted a little amongst all the members of it, of course; besides which the electrical experiments are a main engrosser of one's attention at Bromfield. Add to this that the irregularity of the family habits are a most serious drawback both to my health, & to anything like study there. Wheatstone burst out laughing at the very idea of my having stayed there any time, & yet Wheatstone
is no man of luxuries certainly. He says it is the strangest & most uncomfortable house he ever stayed in.

There are certainly inconceivable oddities there. For instance: the Water-closet can only be got at thro' the Drawing Room; & of course it is perfectly evident the errand one is going on, since the exit leads no where else.

I don't mind that sort of thing the least, when it is inevitable, & I take everything coolly & as a matter of course. That is the only way. Sometimes they lock up the Water Closet, & then one has to make a hue & cry after the key.
Latterly I got into the way of dictating a little,
in a good-humoured way. I used to say I really must ask them to do this or the other at this or that time.
A handsome young lady like me can do these things, but men must take their chance; & I can conceive its being a queer chaos for a many like even Wheatstone.
Now it was almost impossible to set seriously to anything. Thus, I soom determined that I must try & get what I want with young Crosse, at my home.
Wheatstone has given me some very striking counsels. I did not think the little man had such depth in him. I can't write it all to you, or even a small part, but I know you
will agree fully with him, when you do hear it.

The plan for my writing on Babbage's subject, clearly won't do. Don't be vexed at this; a subject is fixed on instead, so it will make no difference, & I can as easily do the one as the other. I have not yet fixed when to leave Town, excepting that on Sunday I go to Esher for 2 nights. I fear my teeth will oblige me to be in Town for 3 days of next week, otherwise I rather think I should not have to return here again for another week or two at soonest.
However I can't exactly say about anything yet. I shall see how I get on between this & Sunday. I find
3. it necessary not to hurry myself, but to take on the contrary plenty of time; or else I am knocked up directly.

The electrical telegraph is laying down on our Southampton Rail, & by & bye Wheatstone says that we shall be able to send any message to Town for a shilling, & get an answer. There will be a secretary at the terminus to write down & dispatch notes to all parts of Town.
For instance, I might from Bridgwater the other day (had the telegraph reached to that station) have sent word in two seconds to London:
"I shall arrive by the two "o'clock Train, instead of the "12 o'clock. Send my carriage for
"the latter hour. A. A. L." Deliver this at 10. St James' Sqre.--

Think what a delight. Wheatstone says that sometimes friends hold conversations from one terminus to the other; that one can send for any one to speak to one. For instance, I might desire a tradesman to go directly to the Nine Elm's Station, & might discuss an order with him about goods &c. Wonderful agent and invention!--
Wheatstone doubts clairvoyance greatly, tho' he believes in all the physical effects of Mesmerism.
Certainly the wonders require to be sifted very rigidly.

Babbage roars with laughter about the state of the Church. He says he never could have conceived such a state of mutual tearing into (not 2 or 3) but hundreds of shreds & atoms. He is uneasy about it, fearing it will all go too fast, which he don't wish. He thinks that we must all turn Church people to stop the crash. That is a joke of his, you understand.--
Today is wretched, everything dreary & bad, a compound of rain, cold &c. This is better however than snow. I never saw London looking more dismal than at present.--
I have written you indeed a long letter, & yet
not a quarter of what I might say.

Wheatstone has fully accounted to me about Herschel & Mrs Woodford. I could not believe that so great a man could seriously admire (I don't mean anything but an intellectual admiration of course) so silly a woman. Wheatstone says that Sir J. H is so affable, so kind, that he never repulses any one; & that he consquently is taken advantage of by silly ladies & silly people of all kinds.
Besides which he is a man of much chivalrous politeness towards women; a politeness of the best kind, & much to Herschel's credit, Wheatstone says.
Mrs. Woodford quite
provoked me by talking so continually of "my poor friend Herschel". I don't like to be too sarcastic, so I forbore; but I was on the point of saying at last, with an apparently naive simplicity,

"Has Sir John Herschel "met with any misfortune "lately?" And when she had stared & answered No, or something of the sort, I should have of course said something playfully witty about the silly use of the word poor.
But I prefer usually holding my peace, & keeping out of the way; tho' I must say that pretension greatly excites me to a sarcastic tone.
I am vexed to find how generally the Queen is disliked for her strange
taste for seeing animals killed.
I cannot make it out. She certainly seems a hard person, in some points.
I don't like some traits I hear, I must say; not from Tories, but from liberal people. I fear she is selfish in certain ways, & about her husband too.
It is difficult to say however.
